title: In Memoriam: Jim Rutt
gist: Obituary of Jim Rutt, former Network Solutions CEO and SFI Board Chair, surfacing his hard-won organizational principles: focus effort on a few important projects, and preserve long-term capability over short-term cuts during crises.

insights.claim: Rutt's hard-won wisdom held that it is far better to focus on a small number of important projects than to disperse attention over numerous small projects that would likely succeed without you.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: A prioritization heuristic for capital and management attention: prune distractions and concentrate on projects that need you.
insights.quote: it is far better to focus on a small number of important projects than feel compelled to disperse one's attention over numerous small projects which are very likely to do well without you
insights.locator: I was very fortunate to have Jim

insights.claim: During the 2008 financial crisis, when many at SFI wanted to fire postdocs and staff, Rutt and Bill Miller were a minority voice arguing to preserve the science at all costs, and leadership credited that choice with avoiding decades of lost recovery.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: A case study in protecting core long-term capability through a downturn rather than cutting to hit near-term numbers.
insights.quote: Jim and [then-Board Chair] Bill Miller were in the minority of voices who said ‘preserve the science at all costs.’

insights.claim: Rutt navigated SFI through one of its most difficult financial periods as Board Chair from 2009 to 2012 under austerity, making decisions that later enabled David Krakauer's recruitment as President in 2015.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Illustrates how disciplined stewardship during a crisis compounds into institutional strength years later.

insights.claim: As CEO of Network Solutions, the company that administered .com, .net, and .org domain namespaces, Rutt negotiated its $15 billion acquisition by Verisign in 2001.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: A concrete valuation marker for early-internet domain infrastructure at the peak of the dot-com era.
insights.quote: In 2001, he negotiated the company's $15 billion acquisition by Verisign.

insights.claim: Rutt was CTO of the Canadian publishing giant Thomson Corporation before running Network Solutions, and discovered complex adaptive systems via John Holland's book while at Thomson.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Background context linking legacy media/publishing leadership to complexity-science thinking.

insights.claim: Rutt worked with Doyne Farmer's group simulating financial markets and conducted research on genetic algorithms and agent-based models as an SFI researcher in residence.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Shows an early crossover between complexity-science modeling and quantitative finance.
insights.locator: By 2002, Rutt was an SFI researcher

insights.claim: Rutt hosted 346 episodes of The Jim Rutt Show since its first episode in July 2019, and his 'Game B' interest was framed as a natural extension of thinking about generative rule systems.
insights.speaker: Santa Fe Institute
insights.so_what: Marks the reach of the Game B / generative-rule-systems discourse in the complexity and alt-governance space.
insights.quote: Since the show’s first episode in July, 2019, with SFI External Professor Simon DeDeo, Rutt hosted 346 episodes.
insights.locator: And later — after he and Celia
